title: Mountains: Geological Function in Scripture
subtitle: Examining scriptural imagery of mountains as Earth's stabilizers against modern geology
summary: Both the Quran and Bible employ poetic language describing mountains as stabilizing forces—the Quran using the metaphor of 'pegs' and 'erection,' while the Bible treats mountains as ancient and enduring. This article examines how these metaphorical descriptions relate to geological reality and explores scholarly interpretations across both traditions.

seo.metaTitle: Mountains as Pegs in Scripture: Quran, Bible, and Geology
seo.metaDescription: How Quranic and Biblical descriptions of mountains relate to plate tectonics and Earth's stability. Explores metaphor vs. literal interpretation in scripture.

sections.title: Introduction: Mountains in Divine Revelation
sections.body:
The Quran describes mountains with images such as 'pegs' and says that mountains were cast into the earth lest it should shift with people. Biblical poetry likewise depicts mountains as ancient, enduring, or foundational features of the created order. These are important theological and literary images, but they should not be turned into the modern geological claim that mountain ranges stop tectonic movement or stabilize the planet.

Modern geology explains mountains through plate collision, subduction, volcanism, faulting, uplift, and erosion. Many major mountain belts are located precisely where crustal deformation and earthquakes are concentrated. This article therefore distinguishes scriptural imagery, premodern interpretation, and present geological knowledge.

sections.title: Quranic Descriptions of Mountains
sections.body: The Quran employs two particularly significant descriptions of mountains. In Surah An-Naba (The Announcement), mountains are described as 'pegs' or 'stakes,' presenting a metaphor of anchoring or stabilization. The Arabic word 'awtad' (أوتاد) refers to pegs driven into the ground to secure tents, establishing a clear image of mountains serving a stabilizing function. In Surah Al-Ghaashiya (The Overwhelming), there is a question directed toward the reader about mountains—inviting contemplation on how they are erected or raised. Both verses appear in Meccan surahs, revealed to an audience familiar with Arabian geography and the visual reality of mountains. Beyond these two verses, a more specific and more directly testable claim appears three times elsewhere in the Quran: 16:15, 21:31, and 31:10 each state that God 'cast into the earth firmly set mountains, lest it shift with you' (or 'with them'). This 'lest it shift' language is the verse set most often cited in the popular argument that the Quran describes a genuine geophysical mechanism—mountains actively preventing the earth from shaking or moving—rather than only a poetic image of permanence. Addressing only the vaguer 'pegs' and 'how are they erected' language would understate the strength of that argument; this article treats the 'lest it shift with you' verses as the claim's strongest and most falsifiable form. The language of all five verses suggests, at minimum, poetic reflection on natural phenomena that inspire awe and draw the observer toward divine consciousness (taqwa), but readers should weigh the 'lest it shift' verses on their own terms rather than only through the softer 'pegs' framing.

sections.title: Biblical Language About Mountains
sections.body: The Bible approaches mountains from theological and poetic perspectives as well. Mountains serve as symbols of permanence, divine presence, and foundational stability in the created order. They appear as places where God meets humanity, from Mount Sinai where the Torah was revealed to Mount Zion as the center of God's earthly kingdom. The Psalms treat mountains as ancient entities that inspire faith and wonder, while the Gospels employ mountain imagery both literally—in accounts of Jesus teaching and transfiguring on mountains—and metaphorically, as when Jesus teaches that faith can 'move mountains.' Habakkuk's prophetic vision describes mountains crumbling before God's majesty, emphasizing their transience compared to divine power. Like the Quranic verses, Biblical mountain references combine observation of natural reality with theological significance.

sections.title: The Geological Reality: Plate Tectonics and Stability
sections.body:
Mountain ranges form through active geological processes. Continental collision thickens and folds crust; subduction produces volcanic arcs; faulting uplifts blocks; and erosion reshapes the resulting relief. Thickened continental crust commonly extends downward beneath high ranges as an isostatic root, but that root is a consequence of crustal buoyancy and mountain building, not a peg that fixes a tectonic plate in place.

Mountains do not prevent earthquakes or stop the crust from moving. The Himalayas, Andes, Alps, and many island arcs lie along active plate boundaries, and the stresses that build mountains also generate major earthquakes and volcanism. Isostasy describes gravitational adjustment of the lithosphere, not a mechanism by which mountains hold the entire earth still.

Mountains nevertheless have important environmental effects. They redirect atmospheric circulation, influence rainfall and rain shadows, store snow and ice, feed rivers, expose diverse rocks and minerals, and create habitats. These contributions to climate and habitability are scientifically defensible but should not be confused with planetary mechanical stabilization.

sections.title: Islamic Scholarly Interpretation: Metaphor and Truth
sections.body: Classical and contemporary Islamic scholars have approached the Quranic description of mountains as 'pegs' with theological sophistication. Many Muslim exegetes recognize the passage as employing metaphorical language—poetic description that conveys truth about mountains' role in stabilizing Earth without claiming to provide geophysical mechanism. Classical and medieval Quranic commentators have discussed the metaphor of pegs as communicating the function of mountains (istiqrar al-ard, the settlement or stabilization of the earth) through comparison with a familiar object from daily life. This approach treats scriptural language as employing rhetorical devices appropriate to its audience and purpose—conveying awareness of divine design rather than scientific exposition. Contemporary scholars emphasize that the Quran's descriptions of natural phenomena are meant to inspire reflection on creation (ayat) and direct believers toward consciousness of the Creator, not to provide a geology textbook. When scholars observe that the tent-peg/pillar cosmology for mountains appears across other ancient Near Eastern texts and that active mountain-building zones are among Earth's most seismically active regions, many contemporary Muslim interpreters respond that this observation is compatible with reading the Quranic language as metaphorical and theological rather than scientific in intent. If one reads the 'peg' language as literal geophysics rather than as theological metaphor, the objections are serious. It is worth noting that the metaphorical reading is itself a matter of historical development rather than a timeless consensus: classical tafsir generally treated the stabilizing function of mountains as literal physical fact within a pre-modern, static-earth cosmology, and the explicitly metaphorical reframing has been particularly emphasized by modern reconciliationist scholars responding to scientific critique, not by a single mainstream Muslim scholarly position holding consistently across all of Islamic history. Proponents of the metaphorical reading argue that the metaphor of pegs conveys a truth about mountains' contribution to Earth's habitability and stability in multiple ways, even though the mechanisms are vastly more complex than literal anchoring. Some modern Muslim scientists and scholars have noted that the Quran's language about mountains as 'pegs' resonates with modern understanding of how mountains contribute to planetary equilibrium, though always through mechanisms of plate tectonics, isostasy, and climate regulation rather than literal anchoring. This interpretation respects both the poetic nature of Quranic language and the validity of scientific investigation into how natural processes actually work.

sections.title: Biblical Hermeneutics: Poetry, Prophecy, and Permanence
sections.body: Christian biblical scholarship has long recognized that much of the Bible employs poetic, prophetic, and metaphorical language. The Psalms, from which much mountain imagery derives, are liturgical poetry designed to evoke emotional and spiritual responses. When Psalm 121 speaks of lifting one's eyes to the hills for help, the passage operates at a symbolic level—mountains as unchanging witnesses to God's faithfulness, not as literal sources of aid. Similarly, when Jesus teaches that faith can move mountains, biblical scholars understand this as hyperbolic language emphasizing the power of faith, not a literal geological claim. Habakkuk's vision of mountains crumbling before God is prophetic and apocalyptic language, conveying the ultimate impermanence of all creation compared to the eternal divine reality. Biblical Hebrew poetry frequently employs hyperbole, metaphor, and parallelism to communicate spiritual truths. Early Christian interpreters developed sophisticated allegorical and typological methods to understand how scriptural language might convey multiple levels of meaning. The broader Christian tradition has never claimed that mountains are literally the primary mechanism by which Earth maintains its stability, nor has it proposed that faith literally moves geological formations. Instead, these images serve theological purposes: communicating God's transcendence over nature, the permanence of divine promises, and the transformative power of faith.

sections.title: Scholarly Approaches: Common Ground and Differing Emphases
sections.body:
Interpreters broadly agree that scriptural mountain language is theological and rhetorically powerful rather than a technical account of plate tectonics. They disagree over whether particular images also anticipate modern findings. A careful compatibility reading may compare 'pegs' with the unseen depth of mountain roots as an analogy, while acknowledging that the texts do not describe isostasy and that roots do not stop tectonic motion.

The stronger claim that modern geology confirms mountains as stabilizers is not supportable. Geological evidence instead associates many mountain belts with ongoing deformation. Theological meaning does not depend on making that scientific claim.

sections.title: Mountains in the Broader Scriptural Message
sections.body: Within both traditions, mountains primarily function as signs of divine power, locations of revelation, images of endurance, and features of a world ordered for life. Their effects on water, climate, landscape, and ecosystems can be discussed accurately. Their theological role is independent of the mistaken idea that they mechanically keep Earth's crust from moving.

quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: And at the mountains - how they are erected?
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: Have We not made the earth a resting place?
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَأَنْهَٰرًۭا وَسُبُلًۭا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And He has cast into the earth firmly set mountains, lest it shift with you, and [made] rivers and roads, that you may be guided,
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِهِمْ وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا فِجَاجًۭا سُبُلًۭا لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And We placed within the earth firmly set mountains, lest it should shift with them, and We made therein [mountain] passes [as] roads that they might be guided.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍۢ تَرَوْنَهَا ۖ وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍۢ ۚ وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءًۭ فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۢ كَرِيمٍ
quranVerses.translation: He created the heavens without pillars that you see and has cast into the earth firmly set mountains, lest it should shift with you, and dispersed therein from every creature. And We sent down rain from the sky and made grow therein [plants] of every noble kind.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: I will lift up my eyes to the hills. Where does my help come from? My help comes from Yahweh, who made heaven and earth.

He said to them, “Because of your unbelief. For most certainly I tell you, if you have faith as a grain of mustard seed, you will tell this mountain, ‘Move from here to there,’ and it will move; and nothing will be impossible for you.
He stood, and shook the earth. He looked, and made the nations tremble. The ancient mountains were crumbled. The age-old hills collapsed. His ways are eternal.

faq.question: Does the Quran claim that mountains are literally pegs that anchor Earth?
faq.answer: Many contemporary Islamic scholars understand the Quranic description of mountains as 'pegs' to be metaphorical language that conveys the idea that mountains stabilize Earth—the purpose of a peg—through poetic expression rather than scientific mechanism. The Arabic word 'awtad' (pegs) is a comparison familiar to the original audience, helping them visualize mountains' stabilizing function. Proponents of this reading argue that it respects both the literary nature of Quranic language and the findings of modern geology. It is worth noting, however, that this metaphorical framing is a more recent interpretive emphasis rather than the uniform historical position—classical commentators often read the stabilizing function more literally—and some critics see the metaphorical reframing as a response prompted by scientific challenge rather than the original, plain sense of the text.

faq.question: Does modern geology confirm that mountains stabilize Earth?
faq.answer: Yes, though through mechanisms that differ from the literal 'peg' metaphor. Mountains contribute to planetary stability through multiple pathways: affecting mass distribution and gravitational fields, regulating climate through precipitation patterns, dissipating tectonic stress, providing sediments for life-sustaining rivers, and creating ecological zones. The geological mechanisms are complex and involve plate tectonics, isostasy, and energy dynamics—not simple physical anchoring.

faq.question: How do Christian scholars interpret Biblical mountain passages?
faq.answer: Christian biblical scholarship recognizes that mountains in Scripture serve primarily theological and metaphorical purposes. Mountains symbolize permanence, divine presence, and God's authority. Biblical poetry (as in the Psalms) uses mountain imagery to inspire faith and worship. When Jesus speaks of 'moving mountains' through faith, this employs hyperbolic language common to both Hebrew and Greek rhetoric to emphasize the power of trust in God, not to make geological claims.

faq.question: Is there a conflict between Scripture's poetic language and scientific geology?
faq.answer: No fundamental conflict exists when Scripture's poetic purpose is understood. Neither the Quran nor the Bible was written to provide geological explanations. Both employ metaphorical language appropriate to their contexts and audiences to convey theological truths. Scientific investigation reveals the mechanisms; Scripture invites contemplation of divine design. A poem about the ocean as 'an infinite mirror' doesn't conflict with physics—it communicates truth through a different language and purpose.

faq.question: Why does both Scripture use mountain imagery if not to teach about geology?
faq.answer: Mountains are prominent features in the natural world experienced by the audiences of these texts. They inspire awe, appear enduring and stable, and serve as boundaries and landmarks. Both the Quran and Bible employ mountains as theological signposts—inviting reflection on divine power, wisdom, and the stability of God's creation. Mountains make excellent vehicles for this spiritual message precisely because they are visibly impressive and seemingly permanent, qualities that serve as reminders of the transcendent.

faq.question: Do Muslim and Christian scholars disagree about the interpretation of these passages?
faq.answer: Within each tradition there is real diversity of opinion, both across history and today. Classical commentators in both traditions often treated mountains' stabilizing and enduring qualities as literal fact within pre-modern cosmologies, while many contemporary scholars—responding in part to modern scientific findings—favor reading these passages as poetic and metaphorical rather than as scientific claims. Both traditions have produced sophisticated interpretive frameworks that allow for scriptural reverence while also engaging scientific investigation. The genuine disagreement, when it occurs, is between literalist and metaphorical approaches, and which reading counts as 'mainstream' has shifted over time rather than being a settled, permanent matter.

furtherReading.title: Where the Conflict Really Lies: Science, Religion, and Naturalism
furtherReading.author: Alvin Plantinga
furtherReading.note: Philosophical analysis examining the relationship between scientific and scriptural language and worldviews, addressing whether apparent conflicts between science and scripture are genuine or stem from methodological confusion (Oxford University Press, 2011).

furtherReading.title: Approaching the Quran: The Early Revelations
furtherReading.author: Michael Sells
furtherReading.note: Literary and philological study of Quranic language and rhetoric, including analysis of how the Quran employs metaphor and poetic devices to communicate theological meaning to its original audience (White Cloud Press, 1999).

furtherReading.title: Plate Tectonics: An Insider's History of the Modern Theory of the Earth
furtherReading.author: Naomi Oreskes (ed.), with Homer Le Grand
furtherReading.note: Historical account of how plate tectonics theory developed and was confirmed, illuminating the actual mechanisms of mountain formation and crustal dynamics (Westview Press, 2001).

furtherReading.title: The Bible and the Believer: How to Read the Bible Critically and Religiously
furtherReading.author: Marc Zvi Brettler, Peter Enns, and Daniel J. Harrington
furtherReading.note: Three scholars—Jewish, evangelical Protestant, and Catholic—each explain how their own tradition reads Scripture in light of historical-critical scholarship, including how metaphorical and non-literal language is understood as authoritative within each community (Oxford University Press, 2012).
